title: 10 Commercial Lease Negotiation Tips for Australian Businesses
metaTitle: 10 Commercial Lease Negotiation Tips for Australian Businesses | Your Office Space
metaDescription: Practical tips to negotiate a better commercial lease in Australia — from starting early to capping rent reviews and getting everything in writing.
excerpt: Most commercial lease negotiations favour the landlord. These 10 tips level the playing field — practical, actionable, and based on real deals across Australia.
author: Joe Kelley
body:
## The 10 tips

Negotiating a commercial lease is not a one-hour job. Done properly, it's a structured process that takes weeks or months and covers dozens of commercial and legal variables. Most tenants go in underprepared and sign a lease that favours the landlord on almost every point.

These 10 tips won't make you an expert — but they'll stop you leaving money on the table.

---

### 1. Start 12 months before your lease expires

This is the single most important thing on this list. If you start looking with 60 days until expiry, you have no leverage. You're choosing between signing whatever the landlord offers or disrupting your business by relocating in a rush.

Start 12 months out. That gives you time to assess the market, explore options, and negotiate properly. Your landlord knows when your lease expires. Make sure you know it first.

### 2. Know the market rate before you negotiate

Don't rely on the landlord's agent to tell you what the market is. They represent the landlord. Find out what comparable spaces in your area are actually leasing for — not just the asking rent, but what deals are being done, with what incentives, and on what terms.

If you don't have access to this data, a tenant rep does.

### 3. Use a tenant representative

A tenant rep works for you, not the landlord. They know the market, know what incentives are available, and negotiate leases every day. The fee typically pays for itself in the first year of the lease through incentives recovered.

You wouldn't send an untrained person to negotiate a complex contract. This is a complex contract.

### 4. Negotiate rent-free at the start

A rent-free period of 1–6 months is standard in most Australian markets. It's built into the landlord's expectations. If you don't ask for it, you won't get it — but if you ask, it's very often available.

On a 500 sqm space at $350/sqm, three months rent-free is worth $43,750. That's real money.

### 5. Cap rent increases

Rent reviews come in several forms: fixed percentage increases (e.g. 3.5% per year), CPI-linked, or market reviews. Market reviews are the riskiest — they can go up significantly and are hard to dispute.

Push for fixed or CPI-capped reviews. If the landlord insists on market reviews, cap the increase at a maximum percentage (e.g. no more than 5% per review). Get it in writing.

### 6. Limit your make-good obligations

The make-good clause at the end of the lease can cost tens of thousands of dollars if it's drafted broadly. "Return to original condition" can mean stripping out your entire fitout.

Negotiate the make-good clause at the start. Define exactly what you're required to do. A well-drafted clause can limit your obligation to painting, carpet replacement, and signage removal — rather than full reinstatement.

### 7. Get a fitout contribution

If the space needs work, ask the landlord to contribute to the fitout cost. A contribution of $50–$150/sqm is common in most markets for quality tenants on reasonable terms. The landlord's interest is in having the space occupied and improved — they have an incentive to help.

Alternatively, ask for a cash contribution (also called a tenant allowance) rather than works done by the landlord's contractor.

### 8. Secure subletting and assignment rights

Your business circumstances may change. You may need to sublet part of the space, or you may want to sell your business and transfer the lease. Without clear subletting and assignment rights in the lease, the landlord can block both.

Negotiate reasonable subletting and assignment rights, including a provision that consent cannot be unreasonably withheld.

### 9. Negotiate a break clause

A break clause allows you to exit the lease early, usually at a specified point (e.g. at year 3 of a 5-year lease) with notice. Landlords don't love break clauses, but they're negotiable — particularly if you're taking a longer initial term.

If your business is growing fast or your needs may change, a break clause protects you from being locked in.

### 10. Get everything in writing before the lease is drafted

All agreed commercial terms — rent, incentives, rent reviews, make-good, fitout contribution, options, break clauses — must be captured in a Heads of Agreement before the lease is prepared. Once the HoA is signed, it sets the framework for the formal lease.

If it's not in the HoA, it may not make it into the lease. Verbal commitments from landlord's agents are worthless.

---

## Final word

These tips will help you negotiate better. But if you want someone who does this every day, talk to us. We represent tenants across Australia, and we know what the market is offering right now.
